worth_it_explanation: Policy issues take ads out of the auction without taking them out of the account, so an ad can sit as disapproved or limited while the campaign still reports it as active. The check is fast. The status column in Google Ads shows every ad and asset that is disapproved, limited, or stuck under review, and Policy Manager groups them by the policy at fault. The fix is usually an edit and an appeal rather than a rebuild, and an ad that comes back into the auction starts serving the day it is approved.
intro: Policy compliance covers whether the account's ads and assets are eligible to serve and whether any policy problems have been worked through rather than left in place. An ad disapproved for a policy reason does not show at all. An ad marked 'Eligible (limited)' shows only in some places or to some audiences. These checks confirm that disapproved and limited ads are being resolved, that repeated violations are not pushing the account toward suspension, and that restricted-content ads carry whatever certification their category requires.

audit_checks.title: Disapproved or 'Eligible (limited)' ads are left unresolved in active campaigns
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A disapproved ad does not enter the auction at all, and an 'Eligible (limited)' ad serves only in a subset of placements or audiences. When the affected ad is the only ad in its ad group, the ad group stops serving while the campaign still reports it as enabled, so spend and volume drop with no obvious cause in the campaign view.

educate.base:
Every ad and asset in Google Ads carries an approval status that controls whether it can serve. An ad reads as 'Approved' when it can run with no restriction, 'Under review' while Google checks it, 'Eligible (limited)' when it complies but can show only in some locations, on some devices, or to some audiences, and 'Disapproved' when its content or its landing page violates a policy and it cannot run.

A disapproved ad is removed from the auction. If it is the only ad in its ad group, the ad group serves nothing, yet the campaign and the ad group both still read as enabled, so the loss is easy to miss from the campaign view. A limited ad keeps serving but only in the placements its restriction allows, which holds back reach against the rest of the account.

Status is not always permanent. Many disapprovals come from an editorial or destination issue that an edit resolves, and the corrected ad can then be appealed for re-review through Policy Manager. Some statuses are informational rather than blocking, so the audit is about separating the ads that are blocked or restricted from the ones that are fine.

A few signs point to unresolved policy issues in the account:
  • The status column shows ads or assets marked 'Disapproved' or 'Eligible (limited)' in campaigns that are still enabled
  • An enabled ad group contains no approved ad, so it cannot serve
  • Policy Manager lists open policy issues with no matching appeal or edit
You may want to read the policy detail on each flagged ad before deciding what to do. The detail names the specific policy, which tells you whether an edit will clear it or whether the placement limit is expected for that product category.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, disapprovals often trace to the product or the landing page rather than the ad text, for example a restricted product, a price or promotion that does not match the page, or a checkout step Google's review cannot reach. You may want to check the flagged ad's destination against the live page since a fix to the page can clear the disapproval without any change to the ad itself.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, disapprovals tend to come from the claims in the ad or the data the form collects, for example unsupported outcome claims or a form that requests sensitive information without the required handling. You may want to confirm the landing page states the offer the ad makes and that the form's fields match what the page describes since a mismatch between the ad, the page, and the form is a frequent cause of a limited or disapproved status.

learn_more.source_title: Check the review status of an ad or asset
Fix a disapproved ad or appeal a policy decision

investigate.intro: Find every ad and asset whose status is disapproved or limited, confirm which enabled ad groups have no approved ad to serve, and read the policy detail behind each flag.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open 'Campaigns', then 'Ads', and add the 'Status' and 'Policy details' columns if they are not already shown.
Filter the ads by status to surface 'Disapproved' and 'Eligible (limited)', and note the campaign and ad group each one sits in.
Hover over a flagged status to read the policy detail, which names the policy at fault and whether the ad was limited or fully disapproved.
For each enabled ad group that contains a flagged ad, check whether any other ad in that group is approved. An enabled ad group with no approved ad is serving nothing.

google_ads_policy_manager.label: Google Ads Policy Manager
steps: Review the 'Policy issues' tab for ads and assets flagged across the account, grouped by the policy involved.
Open the 'Appeal history' tab to see whether the flagged items already have an appeal in progress or whether they have been left without one.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per flagged ad or asset, its status, the policy named in the detail, the campaign and ad group it sits in, and whether an appeal or edit is already in progress. An account where every disapproved or limited item has a matching edit or appeal, and where no enabled ad group is left without an approved ad, passes. An account with disapproved or limited items sitting untouched, or with an enabled ad group that has no approved ad, is a fail recorded with the ad group name and the policy at fault.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the status, the policy detail, and the ad group for each flagged ad above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} has ads or assets sitting in a disapproved or limited status without a resolution in progress. A disapproved ad is held out of the auction, and where it is the only ad in its ad group that ad group serves nothing while the campaign still reads as enabled, so the lost reach does not show up in the campaign view. {{voice_recommend}} reading the policy detail on each flagged item, editing the ad or its landing page to clear the named policy, and appealing the corrected items through Policy Manager. {{voice_recommend}} confirming that every enabled ad group has at least one approved ad so none of them is left unable to serve.

audit_checks.title: Repeated policy violations are accumulating toward account suspension
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Repeated violations of the same policy move an account from individual ad disapprovals toward an account-level suspension, which stops all serving at once. A suspended account is the most severe outcome in the auction since recovery depends on an appeal and reinstatement rather than a single ad edit.

educate.base:
Google Ads treats a single disapproved ad and a pattern of disapprovals differently. One disapproved ad is an item to fix. A pattern of the same violation across many ads, or violations of the policies Google enforces through a warning-and-strike sequence, is treated as an account-level problem that can lead to the whole account being suspended.

An account suspension is the most severe state in the auction. Every campaign stops serving at once, not just the ads that triggered the action, and the account stays down until an appeal is filed and the account is reinstated. The path back runs through review and reinstatement rather than a quick edit, so the time off the auction is measured in the days the appeal takes rather than the minutes an ad edit takes.

The account's policy history is visible before it reaches that point. Policy Manager surfaces the open issues and the appeals tied to them, and Google's compliance tooling flags the policies an account is repeatedly tripping. An account that keeps re-submitting ads that break the same policy, rather than changing the underlying ad or landing page, is the pattern that pushes toward suspension.

You may want to treat a cluster of disapprovals under one policy as a signal to fix the source rather than to appeal each ad in turn. Resubmitting near-identical ads that break the same policy can read as a repeated violation, which works against the account rather than for it.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, a repeated-violation pattern often comes from a catalog where many products share the same policy problem, for example a restricted product type or a claim that recurs across listings. You may want to fix the issue at the catalog or template level rather than ad by ad since correcting one ad while the same problem persists across the rest keeps the pattern alive.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, repeated violations frequently trace to a claim or an offer that appears across many ads and landing pages, for example an outcome promise the page cannot support. You may want to correct the shared claim everywhere it appears since leaving it on other ads keeps the account tripping the same policy even after one ad is fixed.

learn_more.source_title: Our commitment to help you with policy compliance

investigate.intro: Read the account's policy history to see whether the same policy is being violated repeatedly, and check whether any warning or suspension notice is already in place.

google_ads_policy_manager.label: Google Ads Policy Manager
steps: On the 'Policy issues' tab, group the flagged ads by the policy involved and note any policy that recurs across many ads rather than appearing once.
Open the 'Appeal history' tab and look for repeated appeals on the same policy, which can indicate ads being resubmitted without the underlying issue being fixed.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Check the account's notifications and the account status indicator for any policy warning or suspension notice.
If a warning names a specific policy, trace it back to the ads and landing pages that trigger it so the source can be corrected rather than re-submitted.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether any single policy is being violated across many ads, whether the account carries a policy warning or suspension notice, and whether repeated appeals exist on the same policy. An account with no recurring policy violation and no active warning passes. An account with a policy that recurs across many ads, an active warning, or a suspension notice is a fail recorded with the policy named and the count of ads affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record any recurring policy, the account's warning or suspension status, and the count of affected ads above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} is repeatedly violating the same policy across multiple ads, which moves the account toward an account-level suspension rather than a set of individual disapprovals. A suspension stops every campaign at once and is cleared only through an appeal and reinstatement, so the account would be off the auction for as long as that review takes. {{voice_recommend}} fixing the issue at its source, for example the shared claim, product, or landing page that several ads have in common, instead of resubmitting near-identical ads that break the same policy. {{voice_recommend}} working through Policy Manager to confirm the recurring policy is cleared across every affected ad before any further submissions.

audit_checks.title: Restricted-content ads run without the certification their category requires
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Restricted categories such as gambling, healthcare, and financial services require certification or local eligibility before the ads can serve fully. Without it, the ads sit limited or disapproved, so the campaigns serve in fewer places than the budget assumes and reach the intended audience only in part.

educate.base:
Some advertising categories are allowed only under conditions. Google's restricted-content policies cover areas such as alcohol, gambling and games, healthcare and medicines, and financial services, and ads in these categories can serve only where the advertiser meets the requirement for that category and that location. The requirement is often a certification, an allowed-country list, or both.

When an ad falls in a restricted category and the requirement is not met, the ad does not serve everywhere the targeting would otherwise reach. It reads as 'Eligible (limited)' and shows only in the places and to the audiences the policy permits, or it is disapproved outright. The campaign still spends against the placements that remain open, so the limit shows up as weaker reach rather than as a stopped campaign.

The fix depends on the category. Some require applying for and receiving a Google certification before the ads serve fully. Others depend on the targeted countries being ones where the product is allowed. Reading the policy detail on a limited ad tells you which requirement is missing.

You may want to map each restricted-category ad to the requirement its category carries before judging the status. An ad correctly limited to the countries where it is allowed is working as intended, while an ad limited because a certification was never filed is a gap to close.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, restricted-category limits often apply to specific products rather than the whole catalog, for example a supplement, an alcohol product, or a regulated device sold alongside unrestricted items. You may want to isolate the restricted products and confirm each one carries the certification or country eligibility its category needs, rather than treating the catalog as a single policy unit.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation site, restricted limits frequently apply to the service being advertised, for example consumer finance, healthcare, or addiction services, each of which carries its own certification or local requirement. You may want to confirm the advertised service holds the certification its category requires and that the targeted locations are ones where the service is allowed to advertise.

investigate.intro: Identify ads that fall in a restricted category, read why each one is limited, and confirm whether the missing requirement is a certification or a location eligibility.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open 'Campaigns', then 'Ads', and filter to the 'Eligible (limited)' and 'Disapproved' statuses.
Hover over each limited status to read the policy detail and note whether it names a restricted category such as gambling, healthcare, or financial services.
For each restricted-category ad, record whether the detail points to a missing certification or to a location the product is not allowed in.

google_ads_policy_manager.label: Google Ads Policy Manager
steps: Group the flagged ads by policy and isolate the restricted-content categories.
Check whether each restricted category shows a certification requirement and whether that certification is recorded as held or missing for the account.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per restricted-category ad, the category named in the policy detail, the requirement that applies, and whether the certification or country eligibility is in place. An ad whose limit reflects a met requirement, or a deliberate restriction to allowed countries, passes. An ad limited or disapproved because a required certification was never filed, or because it targets countries where the product is not allowed, is a fail recorded with the category and the missing requirement.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the restricted category, the applicable requirement, and its status for each flagged ad above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs ads in a restricted category without the certification or location eligibility that category requires, so those ads are limited or disapproved rather than serving in full. The campaigns keep spending against the placements that stay open, which makes the limit read as weaker reach rather than a stopped campaign, so the budget reaches only part of the intended audience. {{voice_recommend}} mapping each restricted-category ad to its requirement, applying for the certification where one is needed, and confirming the targeted countries are ones where the product is allowed. {{voice_recommend}} rechecking the status after certification so the ads return to full eligibility.
